_meta.description: Rocky Mount's 2026 Lecture Series - Five speakers exploring Tennessee's founding story
_meta.relatedFile: data/events.json - Lectures also appear there for the main calendar
_meta.staffInstructions: HOW TO ADD OR EDIT LECTURES: 1) Copy the template from the '_templates' section below. 2) Fill in the speaker details and lecture information. 3) Add to the 'lectures' array in DATE ORDER. 4) IMPORTANT: Also add this lecture to events.json! Lectures appear in both files. 5) Save and commit your changes.

fieldGuide.id: Sequential number (1, 2, 3...). Just use the next number in sequence.
fieldGuide.title: Lecture title - keep it engaging but informative. This appears as the headline.
fieldGuide.date: Date in YYYY-MM-DD format. Example: '2026-05-15'. Lectures are typically scheduled for Fridays or Saturdays.
fieldGuide.time: Start time. Our lectures typically start at 2:00 PM, but can vary (see David Doan at 11:00 AM).

speaker.name: Full name with title if applicable. Examples: 'Dr. James P. Byrd', 'Lisa Bennett'
speaker.title: Their role or position. Examples: 'Professor', 'Living History Interpreter', 'Historian & Reenactor'
speaker.institution: Where they work or are affiliated. Examples: 'Vanderbilt University', 'Independent Scholar', 'OVTA'
speaker.bio: 2-3 sentences about their background and expertise. What makes them qualified to give this talk?
speaker.portraying: ONLY for first-person interpretation. Example: 'Mary Patton'. Omit this field for standard lectures.

fieldGuide.description: 1-3 sentences summarizing what attendees will learn. Make it compelling - this sells the ticket!
fieldGuide.topics: Array of 3 bullet points highlighting key themes. These appear as a preview list.
fieldGuide.format: Optional - only add if the lecture has a special format like 'First-person interpretation'. Omit for standard lectures.
fieldGuide.note: Optional - any special context like 'Part of Stitching Independence weekend' or connections to other events.

rules: Lectures must be on a day Rocky Mount is open (Wed-Sat)
Keep lectures in DATE ORDER (earliest first)
Every lecture here MUST also be added to events.json with full event details
Speaker bios should be 2-3 sentences max - enough for credibility without being overwhelming
Topics array should have exactly 3 items
Standard lecture time is 2:00 PM unless there's a reason to change it
commonMistakes: Adding a lecture here but forgetting to add it to events.json
Writing overly long bios (keep to 2-3 sentences)
Forgetting to update the id to the next sequential number

checklist: Is the lecture on a day we're open? (Wed-Sat)
Is the id the next number in sequence?
Have you also added this to events.json?
Does the speaker bio establish their credibility?
validation.crossReference: After editing, check events.json to ensure the lecture appears there too with matching details.

speakerTitleExamples.interpreters: Living History Interpreter, Reenactor, Historian & Reenactor

_aiRules.description: Rules for AI assistants editing this file
beforeEditing: ALWAYS verify changes with user before committing
prohibited: Do NOT make changes without user awareness
fileSpecific: Speaker information must be accurate - verify with user
Also update events.json when adding lectures
Cross-reference with events.json for date consistency

_templates._instructions: Copy this template, fill in your details, and add to the 'lectures' array in date order. Remember to also add to events.json!

standardLecture._description: Use for typical academic or expert lectures
standardLecture.title: Your Lecture Title Here

speaker.name: Dr. First Last
speaker.title: Professor
speaker.bio: Write 2-3 sentences about the speaker's background, expertise, and notable works or accomplishments.

standardLecture.description: Write 1-3 compelling sentences about what attendees will learn or experience during this lecture.
topics: First key theme or question the lecture addresses

firstPersonInterpretation._description: Use when the speaker portrays a historical figure in character
firstPersonInterpretation.title: Speaker Name as Character Name: Subtitle

speaker.name: Performer Name
speaker.title: Living History Interpreter
speaker.bio: How long they've portrayed this character, where they've performed, their research background.

firstPersonInterpretation.description: Write 1-3 sentences that capture the historical figure's story and what makes this portrayal special.
topics: A lesser-known aspect of their story

specialEventLecture._description: Use when lecture is part of a larger event weekend
specialEventLecture.title: Your Lecture Title Here

speaker.name: Speaker Name
speaker.title: Their Title
speaker.bio: 2-3 sentences about their background and expertise.

specialEventLecture.description: What will attendees learn? Make it compelling.

_examples._instructions: These are real lectures from our series showing different types. Use them as reference.

academicLecture._explanation: A standard lecture by a university professor. Note the academic credentials in title and bio.

example.title: Colonial Religion on the Frontier

speaker.name: Dr. James P. Byrd
speaker.title: Professor
speaker.bio: Author of 'Sacred Scripture, Sacred War: The Bible and the American Revolution,' Dr. Byrd has appeared on PBS and C-SPAN. He teaches American religious history at Vanderbilt Divinity School.

example.description: What did frontier settlers believe—and why did it matter? Dr. Byrd reveals how Bible, pulpit, and prayer shaped the men who marched to Kings Mountain, turning religious conviction into Revolutionary action.
topics: Faith that moved men to fight

firstPersonLecture._explanation: A living history interpretation where the speaker portrays a historical figure. Note the 'portraying' field and 'format' field.

example.title: Powder for Kings Mountain

speaker.name: Lisa Bennett
speaker.title: Living History Interpreter
speaker.bio: Lisa Bennett has portrayed Mary Patton for over 15 years at Sycamore Shoals and historic sites across the Southeast. Her interpretation is based on extensive research into 18th-century powder production.

example.description: 500 pounds of gunpowder. One determined woman. Without Mary Patton's powder, there would be no victory at Kings Mountain. She walks into the room and tells you her story—face to face, in her own words.
topics: How one woman changed the Revolution
The dangerous art of making gunpowder
Women who stayed—and women who fought

communityHistorianLecture._explanation: A lecture by a community historian/reenactor rather than an academic. Note the different style of bio.

example.title: All In: The Overmountain Men's Gamble

speaker.name: Thomas Bachelor
speaker.title: Historian & Reenactor
speaker.bio: A 20-year veteran of Revolutionary War reenactment and OVTA-affiliated historian, Thomas Bachelor has walked the Overmountain Victory Trail multiple times and published research on the Southern Campaign.

example.description: They bet everything—farms, families, lives. The textbooks call it patriotism. Thomas Bachelor calls it what it was: a desperate gamble by men who knew they might never come home. He strips away the myth.
example.note: Presented one week before Early Frontier Days

series.title: Rocky Mount Lecture Series
series.subtitle: Tennessee's Founding Story
series.description: Five speakers. Five stories that shaped Tennessee. From a woman who made gunpowder to a governor who chose this ground for his capitol—the voices of scholars and living historians bring the 1790s alive. Rocky Mount's first-ever lecture series.
series.note: Advance registration recommended. Seating is limited.

lectures.title: Colonial Religion on the Frontier

speaker.name: Dr. James P. Byrd
speaker.title: Professor
speaker.bio: Author of 'Sacred Scripture, Sacred War: The Bible and the American Revolution,' Dr. Byrd has appeared on PBS and C-SPAN. He teaches American religious history at Vanderbilt Divinity School.

lectures.description: What did frontier settlers believe—and why did it matter? Dr. Byrd reveals how Bible, pulpit, and prayer shaped the men who marched to Kings Mountain, turning religious conviction into Revolutionary action.
topics: Faith that moved men to fight

lectures.title: Powder for Kings Mountain

speaker.name: Lisa Bennett
speaker.title: Living History Interpreter
speaker.bio: Lisa Bennett has portrayed Mary Patton for over 15 years at Sycamore Shoals and historic sites across the Southeast. Her interpretation is based on extensive research into 18th-century powder production.

lectures.description: 500 pounds of gunpowder. One determined woman. Without Mary Patton's powder, there would be no victory at Kings Mountain. She walks into the room and tells you her story—face to face, in her own words.
topics: How one woman changed the Revolution
The dangerous art of making gunpowder
Women who stayed—and women who fought

lectures.title: All In: The Overmountain Men's Gamble

speaker.name: Thomas Bachelor
speaker.title: Historian & Reenactor
speaker.bio: A 20-year veteran of Revolutionary War reenactment and OVTA-affiliated historian, Thomas Bachelor has walked the Overmountain Victory Trail multiple times and published research on the Southern Campaign.

lectures.description: They bet everything—farms, families, lives. The textbooks call it patriotism. Thomas Bachelor calls it what it was: a desperate gamble by men who knew they might never come home. He strips away the myth.
lectures.note: Presented one week before Early Frontier Days

lectures.title: William Blount and the Birth of Tennessee Government

speaker.name: Dr. Caroline H. Whitfield
speaker.title: Historian
speaker.bio: Dr. Whitfield's dissertation examined William Blount's network of political alliances in the Southwest Territory. She has consulted for museums and historic sites across Tennessee.

lectures.description: George Washington sent William Blount west. Blount chose this ground—Rocky Mount—as his capitol. Dr. Whitfield reveals the political intrigue, land speculation, and unlikely friendships that birthed Tennessee.
topics: The road from territory to state
lectures.note: This is Rocky Mount's story—where Tennessee's government began

lectures.title: The Story of the American Flag

speaker.name: David Doan
speaker.title: President, Overmountain Victory Trail Association
speaker.bio: David Doan of Blountville, Tennessee serves as President of the Overmountain Victory Trail Association and Rocky Mount Chapter President. A University of Tennessee Knoxville graduate and eight-year National Guard veteran, his lectures bring Revolutionary War history to life at sites across the region.

lectures.description: Every star and stripe carries a story. From the Grand Union at Boston to the banner that inspired Key's anthem—David Doan traces 250 years of stars and stripes. Followed by Rocky Mount's commemorative flag dedication.
topics: Flags that flew at key battles
